input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the MABI MAX 100 serial robot with six axes, where the first vertical axis at the base is analyzed for joint friction. Key components include the motor, gear transmission system, and cycloidal gear mechanism, which exhibit dynamic behaviors suitable for digital twin modeling to monitor and optimize friction-related performance.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies with SysML for structural modeling across requirements, functional, logical, and product layers. Methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are used for complexity management. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior is described via MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines, with additional detail achieved through integration of MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin constellation follows the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) structured as a UML class diagram (Fig. 1), comprising Asset (physical entities), Model (subtypes: EngineeringModel, DataCalculationModel, SimulationModel), and Processing components responsible for data collection and transformation. This model establishes a one-way data flow from physical assets to digital representations without autonomous feedback mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include geometric representations via SysML IBDs and ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) for component & connector architecture; behavioral models using state machines in SysML v1.7 and MontiArc Automaton; analytical models through MathWorks Simulink and simulation tools for detailed dynamics. Data sources span heterogeneous stakeholders and tools across abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical layers), managed via methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Integration of models in different notations and tools (e.g., C++, Java code) ensures comprehensive system description.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuation system comprises a permanent magnet synchronous motor with current input (IM) and torque output (τM), coupled with a gear transmission followed by a cycloidal gear to achieve a low gear ratio. This configuration enables precise torque control and motion transmission for the robot's first axis, with mechanical constraints defined by the gear ratios and motor specifications.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure includes two position encoders per drive: a motor encoder mounted directly on the motor shaft and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox. This dual-encoder arrangement mitigates backlash and positional uncertainty by providing redundant feedback for accurate motion tracking and control.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is validated through deviation analysis between simulated and measured data, specifically targeting friction loss detection in the robot's first axis. The system addresses limitations in existing friction models by incorporating continuous improvement methods for both white-box (physics-based) and black-box (data-driven) approaches during operation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Automated one-way data flow from physical to digital with sensor data (input current, output angle, torque, angular velocity) streamed via MQTT protocol for live-data linking; data aggregated and abstracted as contextual traces for specific purposes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol used for sensor data streaming; network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering and development processes include structured steps such as problem identification to define objectives, asset and model analysis to evaluate physical components and their digital representations, and data identification to determine required sensor data streams and information sources for deriving concrete Digital Shadows, which inform the creation of active components within a Digital Twin system.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Digital Shadows enable continuous identification of joint friction in robotic systems to improve motion accuracy through real-time analytics, with user interaction facilitated by interactive dashboards and visualization tools for operational decision support.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to industry standards including SysML [17] for Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE), ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25,26] for Architecture Description Languages (ADLs), and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) [17,27], which enable standardized component & connector perspectives and interoperability across heterogeneous stakeholders and tools.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the physical system, identifying the robot model, specific axis analyzed, and key components. It is well-supported by the text. Quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'first vertical robot axis at the base', 'motor... gear transmission... cycloidal gear'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes services provided by the digital shadow, such as improving motion accuracy (optimization) and data visualization in a dashboard. Because relevant information is present in the text, claiming it is missing is incorrect. Supported by quotes: 'improve robot motion accuracy', 'integrated visualization within a unified dashboard'.

The extraction is highly specific and detailed, accurately reflecting the tools and methodologies described in Section II.A of the source. It correctly lists MBSE approaches, specific languages (SysML, ACME, AADL), methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM), and simulation tools (MathWorks Simulink). Quotes: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'methodologies on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP..., OOSEM..., SPES..., or SMArDT', 'combined with further (modeling) tools such as MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java.'

Therefore, the 'Not in Document' extraction is accurate.

The document focuses on a single use case involving one industrial robot axis and a single digital shadow. There is no mention of multiple instances, centralized vs. decentralized architectures, or coordination between multiple twins. Thus, 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction accurately describes the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) presented in the text, correctly identifying the UML structure, the specific components (Asset, Model subtypes, Processing), and the one-way data flow characteristic of a digital shadow. Quotes: 'Fig. 1 presents this refined version of the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram', 'differentiation of models into three types: Engineering models, Data Calculation models, and Simulation models', and 'A digital shadow is characterized by "an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object"'.

While the text mentions technologies like 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' and protocols like 'MQTT', it does not describe integration with external systems, enterprise systems, or interoperability patterns with other Digital Twins as defined by the characteristic. The focus remains on the internal linkage between the robot and its shadow. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is correct.

It does not contain information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy regulations, consent management, or data anonymization. The extraction correctly identifies that this information is missing.

The document describes the hardware setup, noting that 'The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range,' but it does not provide a description of security and safety measures such as cybersecurity protocols, access control, authentication systems, or fail-safe mechanisms as defined by the characteristic. The focus is on motion accuracy and friction modeling rather than system security or safety-critical considerations in the digital twin context. Thus, 'Not in Document' is accurate.
